response:
**Results**

We evaluated ten experimental variants of our proposed method on the validation set, reporting mean validation accuracy and standard deviation over five random seeds for each configuration. The experimental setup included a baseline model (variant_03), which does not incorporate the proposed component, serving as a reference point for assessing the effectiveness of our modifications.

The baseline (variant_03) achieved a mean validation accuracy of 67.8% (±0.6). Among the nine variants incorporating the proposed component, only one configuration, variant_08, surpassed the baseline, yielding a mean accuracy of 70.4% (±0.5). This represents an absolute improvement of +2.6 percentage points over the baseline and demonstrates the potential for substantial gains under appropriate settings.

The remaining eight variants underperformed relative to the baseline. Specifically, variant_01 achieved 67.2% (±0.8), variant_02 66.9% (±0.7), variant_04 65.1% (±0.9), variant_05 66.3% (±1.0), variant_06 64.7% (±0.7), variant_07 65.8% (±0.8), variant_09 66.5% (±0.7), and variant_10 65.9% (±0.8) validation accuracy. These results suggest that, in most configurations tested, the introduction of the proposed component does not yield improvements and may in fact degrade performance.

In summary, our results indicate that while the majority of proposed variants did not outperform the baseline, a single configuration (variant_08) achieved a notable gain in validation accuracy. This finding highlights the importance of careful hyperparameter selection and configuration when deploying the proposed method, as its effectiveness appears sensitive to specific implementation choices.
